trade.summary: Germany is among Norway's most important trading partners. Norway's exports to Germany are dominated by natural gas, oil, fish, and aluminum; Germany exports machinery, vehicles, and chemicals. Germany-Norway energy trade intensified massively after 2022 as Germany sought to replace Russian gas.

diplomatic.status: Close partners, NATO allies, EEA link
diplomatic.summary: Germany and Norway have excellent diplomatic relations. Norway is in the European Economic Area (EEA), giving it deep ties to EU regulatory frameworks while not being an EU member. Both are NATO members. Norway has become Germany's most critical energy partner since 2022 as Germany divested from Russian gas. Cooperation on Arctic policy, fisheries, and climate.

military.summary: Both NATO members with very close military cooperation. Germany participates in NATO's Enhanced Forward Presence in Norway's vicinity and cooperates on high north security. Joint exercises including Cold Response. Norway's geographic position is critical to NATO's northern flank.

financial.summary: Norway's Government Pension Fund Global (GPFG) holds significant German equity and debt. German FDI in Norway focuses on energy and maritime sectors. Norwegian sovereign wealth fund investment in Germany is substantial.

energy.summary: Norway became Germany's largest natural gas supplier after Germany's 2022 phase-out of Russian gas. Norwegian gas via North Sea pipelines (including the recently expanded capacity) is critical to Germany's energy security. Germany also imports Norwegian hydropower indirectly. Offshore wind cooperation in the North Sea is growing rapidly.

scientific.summary: Strong scientific collaboration through European research programs (Norway participates in Horizon Europe). Joint research in ocean science, climate, fisheries, and energy technology. Academic exchanges are significant.
